figures.caption: Fig. 2: Mapped-operator view on one chart. The left side shows the local reference patch (unit ball) with a regular grid; the center arrow represents the chart map 𝜑𝑖 and its Jacobian J𝑖; the right side shows the corresponding physical patch with the grid deformed by the map. The formula cards highlight the three identities the implementation uses directly: gradient pullback, Piola-transformed flux, and normal mapping.

Table 4: Atlas quality diagnostics for the canonical 12-chart rabbit atlas. Membership-filtered evaluates at surface points assigned to each chart; full ball samples the entire reference domain including regions outside the body. Foldover is the fraction of samples with det J𝑖 ≤ 0.

Fig. 6: Representative rabbit-surface visualization for the fixed-atlas SA-PINN solver. The surface is reconstructed from a dense boundary postprocessing pass of the rabbit Poisson solution family and shown from one fixed camera in all three panels. Panel (a) shows the recovered scalar field, panel (b) shows the absolute error, and panel (c) shows the chart entropy induced by the partition-of-unity weights. The first two panels show that the solved field remains smooth on the complex geometry, while the entropy panel shows that uncertainty is confined to narrow overlap traces rather than spreading over the whole surface.

Fig. 7: Rabbit atlas-FEM surface rendering at the finest refinement level (𝑛cells = 56). The figure uses the same reconstructed rabbit surface and the same fixed camera as Figure 6. Panel (a) shows the FEM scalar field, panel (b) shows the absolute error, and panel (c) shows chart entropy reconstructed from the frozen rabbit atlas masks on the shared surface postprocessor. The first two panels show that the atlas-FEM branch recovers the same large-scale field as the compact PINN while producing a visibly smaller surface error.

Fig. 15: Elastoplastic constitutive response. (a) Uniaxial stress–strain curves for three softplus sharpness values 𝜀𝑠; at 𝜀𝑠 = 0.001, the smooth response is indistinguishable from the sharp classical yield point (𝜏𝑦 = 0.5). (b) Cyclic stress–strain loops for three kinematic hardening moduli: 𝐻kin > 0 shifts the reverse yield point (Bauschinger effect), enabling 𝐻kin identification from cyclic data.

Fig. 8: Training diagnostics for the canonical rabbit Poisson compact PINN run. Each panel reports one scalar trace over Schwarz iterations: total objective, PDE residual, boundary loss, interface-value loss, interface-flux loss, and evaluation relative 𝐿2 error.

Fig. 9: Atlas-based FEM convergence on the rabbit benchmark. The dashed line gives an 𝑂 (ℎ2) reference slope; the dotted horizontal line marks the compact PINN accuracy 2.21 × 10−2. The FEM trajectory crosses the PINN level near 𝑛cells = 48.
Table 11: Neural-network families. Configurations are quoted from source code class definitions and canonical checkpoint metadata.

Fig. 5: Ellipsoidal verification benchmark rendered in 3D. The ellipsoid is clipped along a symmetry plane to expose the interior solution field. Panel (a) shows the predicted field 𝑢ℎ, which peaks at the center and vanishes on the boundary as expected for the quadratic exact solution 𝑢∗ = 1 − ∥𝝃∥2. Panel (b) shows the absolute error |𝑢ℎ − 𝑢∗ |, confirming that the operator pullback and the analytic geometric map are implemented correctly (relative 𝐿2 error = 2.26 × 10−3, maximum error = 5.23 × 10−3).

Fig. 14: Schwarz-based distributed parameter identification on the torus, rendered from VTU exports. Top row: tractionmode Schwarz. Bottom row: displacement-mode Schwarz. (a, c) Observation-error magnitude on the torus surface; the traction-mode error is roughly 10× larger than the displacement-mode error. (b, d) Per-chart shear-modulus estimate ?̂?𝑖 mapped onto the torus surface; the color range is [1.79, 1.81] centered on 𝜇true = 1.8. In displacement mode (d), the surface is nearly uniform, consistent with the machine-precision inter-chart consensus (𝜇 std = 1.47× 10−13) reported in Table 9.

Fig. 1: Conceptual view of the volumetric neural atlas. The left side shows chart-local reference patches (unit balls) parameterized by 𝜻 ; the right side shows their mapped physical images covering overlapping parts of Ω. Solid arrows represent the chart maps 𝜑𝑖; dashed arrows represent the local inverses 𝜋𝑖 . The lower formula indicates the global blend assembled from the chart-local fields. The drawing is a planar slice through a three-dimensional construction.
Table 5: Atlas-based FEM ℎ-refinement study for the rabbit Poisson benchmark. Rates are computed between successive refinement levels using the relative-𝐿2 error.

Fig. 12: Torus inverse benchmark (global optimizer), rendered from VTU exports on the three-dimensional surface. (a) Prescribed deformed configuration colored by displacement magnitude |u|; the torsional load is localized over a ∼126◦ azimuthal arc. (b) Traction-error magnitude |t − tobs |; the mismatch is concentrated in the loaded region and remains below 1.2 × 10−3 everywhere. (c) Eight-chart atlas decomposition of the torus, with each color denoting a distinct azimuthal chart; the multi-chart atlas is topologically necessary because the solid torus is not simply connected.

Fig. 13: Parameter convergence history for the global torus optimizer. (a) Relative errors of 𝜇 and 𝐾 decrease monotonically over 100 epochs, reaching ∼10−2% for both parameters. (b) Traction mismatch Ltrac decreases by five orders of magnitude from initialization to the 10−8 level. The oscillatory pattern in (a) reflects the alternating descent directions in the two-parameter landscape.

Fig. 11: Displacement field components on the torus at step 100 of the cyclic loading protocol (𝑛cells = 10, 𝛿 ≈ − 2 3 𝛿max, recovery phase). (a) 𝑢𝑥 , (b) 𝑢𝑦 , (c) 𝑢𝑧 . The opposite 𝑥-displacement boundary conditions at 𝜙 ≈ 0 and 𝜙 ≈ 𝜋 produce the dominant 𝑢𝑥 pattern, while 𝑢𝑦 and 𝑢𝑧 arise from the curved torus geometry coupling in-plane compression to outof-plane deformation. All fields are the direct P1 finite-element nodal solution with no postprocessing interpolation.

Fig. 10: Diagnostics for the 8-chart Schwarz forward elastoplastic BVP. (a) Accumulated plastic strain 𝜀𝑝 vs. load step for three mesh resolutions; finer meshes resolve larger peak strains near the loaded sections. (b) Schwarz interface displacement jump remains 𝑂 (10−7) throughout. (c) Load-step convergence: the final plastic strain varies by 0.16% across a 4× range of step sizes. (d) Computational cost scaling with total DOF.

Fig. 3: Conceptual and algorithmic view of fixed-atlas SA-PINN coupling. Panel (a) shows two overlapping chart images inside the physical body. The overlap Ω𝑖 𝑗 induces the artificial interfaces Γ𝑖 𝑗 and Γ 𝑗𝑖 on which neighboring chart solutions exchange temporary transmission data during training. Panel (b) shows one multiplicative Schwarz sweep: the solver snapshots the full chart state, updates one active chart or non-overlapping color group while neighbor states are detached, evaluates global residual and interface diagnostics, and then accepts the sweep or restores the previous state.

Fig. 18: Sensitivity studies. (a) 𝜏𝑦 identification error versus mesh resolution; accuracy is consistent across all mesh sizes (𝑛cells ∈ {3, 4, 6, 8}). (b) 𝜏𝑦 convergence trajectories for different initial smoothing parameters 𝜀𝑠,start; all values except 𝜀𝑠,start = 1.0 converge to the correct yield stress.

Fig. 16: Inverse parameter recovery convergence. (a) Stage 1: 𝜏𝑦 trajectories from six initial guesses; all converge to 𝜏𝑦,true = 0.5 within 100 iterations. (b) Stage 2: 𝐻kin recovery from 𝐻kin,0 = 5 using reaction-force observations; the estimate reaches 19.58 (2.11% error) after 150 iterations.

Fig. 17: Recovered versus true cyclic stress–strain response at three sample locations on a single torus chart sector. At all three locations the hysteresis loops generated by the true parameters (solid) and the identified parameters (dashed) are visually indistinguishable.

Table 1: Notation used in Section 2. Physical-space variables, chart-local reference variables, and global reference-ball variables are separated explicitly so that the reader can track which coordinate system is active at each step of the derivation.

Fig. 4: Atlas quality verification for the 12-chart rabbit atlas. (a) Jacobian determinant distributions per chart evaluated at membership-filtered points; all charts maintain det J𝑖 > 0. (b) Condition number 𝜅(J𝑖) box plots per chart; median values range from 1.8 to 6.1. (c) Overlap degree distribution: 80% of surface points lie in exactly one chart. (d) Pairwise transition-map consistency error ∥𝜑𝑖 − 𝜑 𝑗 ∥ (mean across 37 overlapping pairs: 2.62 × 10−2).
